title: Podman machine will not operate as root
body:
I only found one previous visible instance of this: https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/20011

Why exactly is podman machine prohibited from being used as root?

This **is** superficially documented: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/1492cea16e08df16417c5256ac82e7bc442a2c2e/docs/source/markdown/podman-machine.1.md?plain=1#L17

Git blame on the documentation line shows: https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/d9ff0fd15da268fae9744a60522ecdf94744b74a

```
Podman Machine crashes if run as root. When creating the machine,
we write the ignition so that the UID of the core user matches the UID
of the user on the host. We by default, create the root user on the machine
with UID 0. If the user on the host is root, the core UID and the Root UID
collide, causing a the VM not to boot.
```

I guess the commit message explains why this limitation was set.

Should this remain the situation going forward?

As a user, it would be desirable for me to be able to use podman identically independent of the UID I'm running under. (I have some systems which are essentially single-user.) UID 0 should be usable the same as any other UID, perhaps with less security restrictions, or increased reliance on other restriction mechanisms. Podman would (? - but this is a different discussion) also be easier to user in nested containers as the root user. - This is probably the more common case of podman under UID 0.

comments.body:
This was mostly a technical limitation on how we initialize and boot the machine. As you found in the comment, we wanted to map the inside and outside of the machine to work as seamlessly as possible, so we made the UID of the core user match the UID of the user on the host. However, only podman machine commands are restricted to running rootless. After starting/configuring the machine, you're still able to run root containers if you use the root connection on the machine; we don't prevent root containers.

I think the main issue in allowing this is if you initialize a machine as root, what do we do for the core user? We could generate a random UID but I feel like that would cause errors later on if someone did try to use a rootless connection later. IIRC we need to have the core user in the VM too, we can't just have root only.

I guess there can also be some security considerations, I am not sure tools like gvproxy or maybe even qemu have enough sandboxing when started as root so it is really best to not start these things with privileges to being with as we really do not need them. But I guess that can be argued to be the users issue and not a reason to block it
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
